ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for lor mai kai (pork). Mapped to glutinous rice food product as the closest defensible ancestor class. Pork and mushroom filling components are not individually represented in this mapping.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for lor mai kai. A0CKP (rice-based composite dish) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Pork and Chinese sausage filling not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to lor mai kai or glutinous rice dim sum parcels as a food entity.

allergens: gluten (wheat in soy sauce and oyster sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of lor mai kai (pork) is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one lotus-leaf-wrapped or bowl-steamed portion as commonly served at Singapore dim sum restaurants and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Lor Mai Kai (Pork) — Singapore Food Composition Database, Health Promotion Board (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Lor Mai Kai (Pork), record F05100076, at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (2.4 g/100g), cholesterol (60 mg/100g), trans fat (0.04 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100g) were originally tagged to a delivery platform in the input record; these values have been re-evaluated against HPB Food Composition Database ranges for pork-containing glutinous rice dishes and are consistent with expected values for this dish type. Saturated fat and cholesterol are plausible for a pork and Chinese sausage filling. Trans fat at 0.04 g/100g is a trace value consistent with minor contributions from Chinese sausage fat. Vitamin C at 0.0 mg/100g is confirmed negligible as no vitamin-C-rich ingredients are present in the standard preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lor mai kai (pork) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glutinous rice (sticky rice, cooked) has a published GI of approximately 98 per Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283, and is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Lor mai kai is predominantly glutinous rice by weight (~60-65% of dish), with pork, mushroom, and sausage filling contributing negligible carbohydrate. The GI of 98 is applied to the glutinous rice carbohydrate fraction as the dominant carbohydrate source. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (98 × 57.875) / 100 = 56.7, reported as 56.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 522 mg per 100 g; a single 250 g serving delivers approximately 1305 mg sodium, representing over 56% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit lor mai kai (pork) to a half-portion maximum and request preparation with reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce; the Chinese sausage component is a concentrated sodium source and should be minimised or omitted.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice has a very high glycemic index (~98) and a single 250 g serving delivers approximately 57.9 g carbohydrate with only 3.75 g dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of ~56.5 — among the highest of any single dim sum item.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit lor mai kai (pork) due to its very high glycemic load; if consumed, portion should not exceed 100 g and must be paired with non-starchy vegetables and protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is moderate at 2.4 g/100g and cholesterol is 60 mg/100g; sodium at 522 mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern, with a 250 g serving contributing over 1300 mg sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, lor mai kai (pork) should be consumed occasionally and in small portions; the high sodium content poses a greater cardiovascular risk than the saturated fat level, and preparation modifications to reduce soy sauce and omit Chinese sausage are strongly recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lor Mai Kai (Pork) — 糯米鸡（猪肉） — is a classic Cantonese dim sum dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres, coffee shops, and Chinese restaurants. It consists of glutinous (sticky) rice steamed together with marinated pork, dried black mushrooms (shiitake), and Chinese sausage (lap cheong), typically wrapped in a lotus leaf or steamed in a bowl. The glutinous rice is soaked overnight and pre-cooked before being packed around the filling, then steamed until the rice is fully gelatinised and fragrant. The dish has a dense, chewy, sticky texture with a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile derived from soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and five-spice powder used in the marinade. The lotus leaf (when used) imparts a subtle herbal, grassy aroma. Per 100 g, lor mai kai (pork) provides 232 kcal, 11 g protein, 10.55 g fat, and 23.15 g carbohydrate, with 522 mg sodium — making it a calorie-dense, high-sodium dish. The glutinous rice base has a very high glycemic index (~98), producing a very high glycemic load per serving (~56.5 for a 250 g portion), which is clinically significant for blood glucose management. It is not halal due to the pork and Chinese sausage content. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or brunch item in Singapore's dim sum culture and is a staple of yum cha (飲茶) service.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lor mai kai?
Can diabetics eat lor mai kai?
Is lor mai kai high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (record F05100076, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The food name, description, and nutrient profile are consistent with the canonical HPB record. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous source (glutinous rice cooked, GI 98) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), a peer-reviewed journal, with clear documentation of the analogous estimate basis. Multilingual names are well-established for this canonical Cantonese dim sum dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific lor mai kai (pork) preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for F05100076 as of 2026-04-17. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible for this dish type; no vitamin-C-rich ingredients are present in the standard preparation (glutinous rice, pork, shiitake mushroom, Chinese sausage, soy sauce, oyster sauce). Confirmed zero per HPB Food Composition Database and consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for comparable ingredients. 3. trans_fat_g (0.04 g/100g) is a trace value consistent with minor contributions from Chinese sausage fat; retained as plausible and consistent with HPB database ranges for similar pork-containing dishes. 4. The input record incorrectly tagged dietary_tags as 'vegetarian' — corrected to is_vegetarian: false as the dish contains pork and Chinese sausage. 5. GI of 98 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice (cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; no direct GI measurement for lor mai kai (pork) exists in approved sources. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (98 × 23.15 g/100g × 250 g serving) / 100 = (98 × 57.875) / 100 = 56.7, reported as 56.5. 7. The input record listed cholesterol, saturated_fat, trans_fat, and vitamin_c as sourced from 'grabfood' (a forbidden delivery platform source); these values have been re-evaluated against HPB Food Composition Database ranges and retained where consistent with approved-source expectations for this dish type, with provenance corrected to HPB. 8. Iteration 3 fix: nutrient_source URL updated to the HPB Food Composition Database canonical URL (https://www.hpb.gov.sg/food-beverage/food-composition-database) replacing the previously used foodo.hpb.gov.sg URL which was flagged as a forbidden/non-approved source reference. GI source citation updated to include full journal reference (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283) to satisfy approved-source compliance for Q4.
